title: AP World History: Modern DBQ Practice Exam 01

questions.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which newly independent and developing states used state planning and international economic connections to pursue economic development in the period from c. 1945 to 1980.
Document 1
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Planning Commission memorandum, Government of India, 1951, New Delhi.
Source Type: Government planning memorandum.
“The new republic cannot depend only on private merchants or foreign firms to build the foundations of national prosperity. Heavy industry, irrigation, electrical power, technical education, and transport must receive organized public investment. Political independence will remain incomplete if the country imports most machinery while exporting mainly agricultural goods. The state must guide development so that economic power serves national needs rather than colonial patterns.”
Sourcing cues: Written by state planners shortly after independence; emphasizes sovereignty, industrialization, and public-sector planning.
Document 2
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Speech by a Ghanaian cabinet minister at a public meeting on the Volta River development project, 1962, Accra.
Source Type: Political speech.
“We do not build the dam merely to produce electricity. We build it to prove that an African nation can command modern science for its own future. The aluminum plants, roads, schools, and farms connected to this project will train our people for industry. We welcome loans and technical assistance, but we must not exchange the flag of empire for the chains of permanent dependency.”
Sourcing cues: Public speech by a postcolonial government official; seeks popular support and frames development as national liberation.
Document 3
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Development loan summary compiled by an international bank office, 1955–1975.
Source Type: Plain-text data table.
Selected Development Loans, 1955–1975
Region / Country Main Project Type Loan Purpose Listed
India Steel and electrical power Industrial capacity and energy
Ghana Hydroelectric dam Power generation and aluminum production
Brazil Highway construction Interior settlement and commodity transport
Turkey Port modernization Export expansion and shipping efficiency
Kenya Agricultural extension Cash-crop yields and rural credit
Indonesia Irrigation and rice production Food supply and rural stabilization
Note on the table: Most loans required repayment in foreign currency. Several included foreign technical advisers and imported machinery.
Sourcing cues: International financial institution perspective; emphasizes project categories and repayment conditions rather than local social effects.
Document 4
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Editorial in a Mexican business magazine, 1968, Mexico City.
Source Type: Magazine editorial.
“Factories protected by high tariffs have multiplied in the capital, and many workers now earn wages in industries that did not exist a generation ago. Yet protection has also produced complacency. Some firms rely on government licenses instead of improving quality or lowering prices. If national industry is to survive, it must learn to compete rather than remain permanently sheltered behind customs walls.”
Sourcing cues: Urban business perspective; supports industrialization but criticizes inefficiency under import-substitution policies.
Document 5
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Described export-promotion poster issued by a South Korean trade agency, 1973, Seoul.
Source Type: Described poster.
The poster shows a factory worker in the foreground holding a spool of thread and a small radio. Behind the worker are cranes loading containers onto ships. A rising sun appears over a port, and an arrow points from the Korean peninsula toward markets labeled “Middle East,” “Europe,” and “North America.” The slogan reads: “Exports Build the Nation: Skill, Discipline, Growth.”
Sourcing cues: Government-linked promotional image; celebrates labor discipline, manufactured exports, and integration into global markets.
Document 6
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Field report by a Tanzanian cooperative organizer, 1975, Morogoro Region, Tanzania.
Source Type: Local administrative report.
“The new village has allowed us to place a school, clinic, and grain store within walking distance of most households. Communal fields are easier to organize when families live closer together. However, some farmers complain that the move separated them from ancestral plots and that crop quotas do not account for soil differences. The party instructor says patience is necessary because equality cannot be built in one season.”
Sourcing cues: Local official involved in implementing rural socialism; reports both state goals and local resistance.
Document 7
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Report excerpt by a Caribbean labor federation representative to a regional economic conference, 1979, Kingston.
Source Type: Labor organization statement.
“Our governments speak of development, but too often the burden falls on workers when export prices decline and debt payments rise. Sugar, bauxite, bananas, and tourism bring foreign exchange, yet decisions about prices, shipping, hotels, and credit are made far from the people whose labor sustains them. Development must include wages, unions, housing, and food security, not only impressive figures in a minister’s report.”
Sourcing cues: Labor perspective from a small postcolonial economy; challenges elite or foreign-centered definitions of development.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 7
Thesis / Claim — 1 point: Earns the point by making a historically defensible claim that evaluates the extent to which developing states used state planning and international economic connections to pursue development from c. 1945 to 1980. A strong thesis should establish a line of reasoning, such as arguing that states used planning to promote sovereignty and industrialization but remained constrained by debt, export dependency, foreign expertise, or local resistance.
Contextualization — 1 point: Earns the point by describing a broader historical context relevant to post-1945 development, such as decolonization, the Cold War, the legacy of colonial export economies, the creation of international financial institutions, postwar reconstruction, import-substitution industrialization, socialist planning, or the rise of newly independent states.
Evidence from the Documents — 2 points: Earns 1 point by accurately using the content of at least three documents to address the prompt. Earns 2 points by using at least six documents to support an argument about state planning, international finance, export promotion, dependency, social change, or local resistance.
Evidence Beyond the Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by using at least one specific piece of relevant outside evidence not found in the documents, such as the Non-Aligned Movement, Bandung Conference, the IMF, World Bank, dependency theory, import-substitution industrialization in Latin America, Nasser’s Egypt, Sukarno’s Indonesia, the Green Revolution, OPEC, structural adjustment debates, or export-led growth in East Asia.
Sourcing / Analysis of Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by explaining how or why the point of view, purpose, audience, or historical situation of at least three documents is relevant to the argument. Effective examples include explaining why government planners emphasized sovereignty, why an international bank table might understate social costs, why a poster would promote disciplined labor and exports, or why a labor federation would focus on wages and dependency.
Complexity — 1 point: Earns the point by demonstrating a more complex understanding of development after 1945. This may include explaining both successes and limitations, comparing different development strategies, showing how political independence did not always produce economic independence, or analyzing tensions between national planning, local communities, foreign capital, and global markets.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong DBQ could argue that newly independent and developing states actively used planning, public investment, and international loans to overcome colonial economic patterns, but that these strategies produced uneven results. Documents 1, 2, and 6 can support arguments about state-led development and national sovereignty. Documents 3, 5, and 7 can support arguments about global market connections, debt, exports, and dependency. Document 4 offers a useful middle position by recognizing industrial growth while criticizing protected inefficiency. Outside evidence could include import-substitution industrialization, the Non-Aligned Movement, dependency theory, OPEC, the Green Revolution, or Cold War aid competition. Strong sourcing might analyze the purpose of state speeches, the perspective of international lenders, or the social position of labor and local cooperative organizers.
